solution:
(from \url{artofproblemsolving.com})
We will prove that the sum converges to $\pi^2/16$.
Note first that the sum does not converge absolutely, so we are not free to rearrange it arbitrarily. For that matter, the standard alternating sum test does not apply because the absolute values of the terms does not decrease to 0, so even the convergence of the sum must be established by hand.

Setting these issues aside momentarily, note that
the elements of the set counted by $A(k)$ are those odd positive integers $d$ for which $m = k/d$ is also an integer and $d < \sqrt{2dm}$; if we write $d = 2\ee-1$, then the condition on $m$ reduces to $m \geq \ee$. In other words, the original sum equals
\[
S_1 := \sum_{k=1}^\infty \sum_{{\ee \geq 1, m \geq \ee}\atop{k = m(2\ee-1)}} \frac{(-1)^{m-1}}{m(2\ee-1)},
\]
and we would like to rearrange this to
\[
S_2 := \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{m=\ee}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{m-1}}{m},
\]
in which both sums converge by the alternating sum test. In fact a bit more is true:
we have
\[
\left| \sum_{m=\ee}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{m-1}}{m} \right| < \frac{1}{\ee},
\]
so the outer sum converges absolutely.
In particular, $S_2$ is the limit of the truncated sums
\[
S_{2,n} = \sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq n} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{m=\ee}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{m-1}}{m}.
\]
To see that $S_1$ converges to the same value as $S_2$, write
\[
S_{2,n} - \sum_{k=1}^n (-1)^{k-1} \frac{A(k)}{k} =
\sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq n} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{m=\lfloor \frac{n}{2\ee-1}+1 \rfloor}^\infty
\frac{(-1)^{m-1}}{m}.
\]
The expression on the right is bounded above in absolute value by the sum $\sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq n} \frac{1}{n}$, in which the number of summands is
%at most $\sqrt{n/2}$ and so the total is bounded by $1/\sqrt{2n}$.
at most $\sqrt{n}$ (since $\sqrt{n}(2\sqrt{n}-1)\geq n$), and so the total is bounded above by $1/\sqrt{n}$.
Hence the difference converges to zero as $n \to \infty$; that is, $S_1$ converges and equals $S_2$.

We may thus focus hereafter on computing $S_2$. We begin by writing
\[
S_2 = \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{m=\ee}^\infty (-1)^{m-1} \int_0^1 t^{m-1}\,dt.
\]
Our next step will be to interchange the inner sum and the integral, but again this requires some justification.
\begin{lemma}
Let $f_0, f_1, \dots$ be a sequence of continuous functions on $[0,1]$ such that for each $x \in [0,1]$, we have
\[
f_0(x) \geq f_1(x) \geq \cdots \geq 0.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{n=0}^\infty (-1)^n \int_0^1 f_n(t)\,dt = \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{n=0}^\infty (-1)^n f_n(t) \right)\,dt
\]
provided that both sums converge.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
Put $g_n(t) = f_{2n}(t) - f_{2n+1}(t) \geq 0$; we may then rewrite the desired equality as
\[
\sum_{n=0}^\infty \int_0^1 g_n(t) \,dt = \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{n=0}^\infty g_n(t) \right)\,dt,
\]
which is a case of the Lebesgue monotone convergence theorem.
\end{proof}
By Lemma~1, we have
\begin{align*}
S_2 &= \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{m=\ee}^\infty (-1)^{m-1} t^{m-1} \right) \,dt \\
&=  \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \int_0^1 \frac{(-t)^{\ee-1}}{1+t}  \,dt.
\end{align*}
Since the outer sum is absolutely convergent, we may freely interchange it with the integral:
\begin{align*}
S_2 &=  \int_0^1 \left(
\sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1}  \frac{(-t)^{\ee-1}}{1+t} \right)\,dt \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{t}(1+t)} \left( \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{\ee-1} t^{\ee-1/2}}{2\ee-1} \right) \,dt \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{t}(1+t)} \arctan(\sqrt{t})\,dt \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{2}{1+u^2} \arctan(u)\,du \qquad (u = \sqrt{t}) \\
&= \arctan(1)^2 - \arctan(0)^2 = \frac{\pi^2}{16}.
\end{align*}

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
For each positive integer $indexvar$, let $oddiviscount(indexvar)$ be the number of odd divisors of $indexvar$ in the interval $[1, \sqrt{2\,indexvar})$. Evaluate
\[
\sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty} (-1)^{indexvar-1} \frac{oddiviscount(indexvar)}{indexvar}.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
We will prove that the sum converges to $\pi^2/16$.
Note first that the sum does not converge absolutely, so we are not free to rearrange it arbitrarily. For that matter, the standard alternating sum test does not apply because the absolute values of the terms do not decrease to $0$, so even the convergence of the sum must be established by hand.

Setting these issues aside momentarily, note that the elements of the set counted by $oddiviscount(indexvar)$ are those odd positive integers $divisvar$ for which $multivar = indexvar/divisvar$ is also an integer and $divisvar < \sqrt{2\,divisvar\,multivar}$; if we write $divisvar = 2\ee-1$, then the condition on $multivar$ reduces to $multivar \geq \ee$. In other words, the original sum equals
\[
firstseries := \sum_{indexvar=1}^{\infty} \sum_{\substack{\ee \ge 1,\\ multivar \ge \ee} \atop {indexvar = multivar(2\ee-1)}} \frac{(-1)^{multivar-1}}{multivar(2\ee-1)},
\]
and we would like to rearrange this to
\[
secondseries := \sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{multivar=\ee}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{multivar-1}}{multivar},
\]
in which both sums converge by the alternating-sum test. In fact a bit more is true:
\[
\Bigl|\,\sum_{multivar=\ee}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{multivar-1}}{multivar}\,\Bigr| < \frac{1}{\ee},
\]
so the outer sum converges absolutely. In particular, $secondseries$ is the limit of the truncated sums
\[
secondtruncate = \sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \le \truncvar} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{multivar=\ee}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{multivar-1}}{multivar}.
\]
To see that $firstseries$ converges to the same value as $secondseries$, write
\[
secondtruncate 
- \sum_{indexvar=1}^{\truncvar} (-1)^{indexvar-1} \frac{oddiviscount(indexvar)}{indexvar}
= \sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \le \truncvar} \frac{1}{2\ee-1}
 \sum_{multivar=\lfloor \frac{\truncvar}{2\ee-1}+1 \rfloor}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{multivar-1}}{multivar}.
\]
The expression on the right is bounded above in absolute value by the sum $\sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \le \truncvar} \frac{1}{\truncvar}$, in which the number of summands is at most $\sqrt{\truncvar}$ (since $\sqrt{\truncvar}\,(2\sqrt{\truncvar}-1) \ge \truncvar$), and so the total is bounded above by $1/\sqrt{\truncvar}$. Hence the difference converges to $0$ as $\truncvar \to \infty$; that is, $firstseries$ converges and equals $secondseries$.

We may thus focus hereafter on computing $secondseries$. We begin by writing
\[
secondseries 
= \sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{multivar=\ee}^{\infty} (-1)^{multivar-1} \int_{0}^{1} paramvar^{multivar-1}\,dparamvar.
\]
Our next step will be to interchange the inner sum and the integral, but again this requires some justification.

\begin{lemma}
Let $seriesf_0, seriesf_1,\dots$ be a sequence of continuous functions on $[0,1]$ such that for each $realvar \in [0,1]$,
\[
seriesf_0(realvar) \ge seriesf_1(realvar) \ge \cdots \ge 0.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{\truncvar=0}^{\infty} (-1)^{\truncvar} \int_{0}^{1} seriesf_{\truncvar}(paramvar)\,dparamvar 
= \int_{0}^{1} \Bigl(\sum_{\truncvar=0}^{\infty} (-1)^{\truncvar} seriesf_{\truncvar}(paramvar)\Bigr)\,dparamvar,
\]
provided that both sums converge.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
Put $seriesg_{\truncvar}(paramvar)=seriesf_{2\truncvar}(paramvar)-seriesf_{2\truncvar+1}(paramvar)\ge0$; we may then rewrite the desired equality as
\[
\sum_{\truncvar=0}^{\infty} \int_{0}^{1} seriesg_{\truncvar}(paramvar)\,dparamvar 
= \int_{0}^{1} \Bigl(\sum_{\truncvar=0}^{\infty} seriesg_{\truncvar}(paramvar)\Bigr)\,dparamvar,
\]
which is a case of the Lebesgue monotone-convergence theorem.
\end{proof}

By Lemma 1,
\begin{align*}
secondseries &= \sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \int_{0}^{1} \Bigl(\sum_{multivar=\ee}^{\infty} (-1)^{multivar-1} paramvar^{multivar-1}\Bigr)\,dparamvar\\
&= \sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{(-paramvar)^{\ee-1}}{1+paramvar}\,dparamvar.
\end{align*}
Since the outer sum is absolutely convergent, we may freely interchange it with the integral:
\begin{align*}
secondseries &= \int_{0}^{1} \Bigl(\sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \frac{(-paramvar)^{\ee-1}}{1+paramvar}\Bigr)\,dparamvar\\
&= \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{paramvar}\,(1+paramvar)} \Bigl(\sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{\ee-1} paramvar^{\ee-1/2}}{2\ee-1}\Bigr)\,dparamvar\\
&= \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{\sqrt{paramvar}\,(1+paramvar)}\arctan(\sqrt{paramvar})\,dparamvar\\
&= \int_{0}^{1} \frac{2}{1+sqrtvar^{2}}\arctan(sqrtvar)\,d sqrtvar \qquad (sqrtvar=\sqrt{paramvar})\\
&= \arctan(1)^{2}-\arctan(0)^{2}=\frac{\pi^{2}}{16}.
\end{align*}

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
For each positive integer $kangaroo$, let $appetite(kangaroo)$ be the number of odd divisors of $kangaroo$ in the interval $[1, \sqrt{2kangaroo})$. Evaluate
\[
\sum_{kangaroo=1}^{\infty} (-1)^{kangaroo-1} \frac{appetite(kangaroo)}{kangaroo}.
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We will prove that the sum converges to $\pi^2/16$.
Note first that the sum does not converge absolutely, so we are not free to rearrange it arbitrarily. For that matter, the standard alternating sum test does not apply because the absolute values of the terms does not decrease to 0, so even the convergence of the sum must be established by hand.

Setting these issues aside momentarily, note that
the elements of the set counted by $appetite(kangaroo)$ are those odd positive integers $daffodil$ for which $marigold = kangaroo/daffodil$ is also an integer and $daffodil < \sqrt{2daffodil marigold}$; if we write $daffodil = 2\ee-1$, then the condition on $marigold$ reduces to $marigold \geq \ee$. In other words, the original sum equals
\[
sandstone := \sum_{kangaroo=1}^{\infty} \sum_{{\ee \geq 1, marigold \geq \ee}\atop{kangaroo = marigold(2\ee-1)}} \frac{(-1)^{marigold-1}}{marigold(2\ee-1)},
\]
and we would like to rearrange this to
\[
sunflower := \sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{marigold=\ee}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{marigold-1}}{marigold},
\]
in which both sums converge by the alternating sum test. In fact a bit more is true:
we have
\[
\left| \sum_{marigold=\ee}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{marigold-1}}{marigold} \right| < \frac{1}{\ee},
\]
so the outer sum converges absolutely.
In particular, sunflower is the limit of the truncated sums
\[
sunflowerpetal = \sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq nectarine} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{marigold=\ee}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{marigold-1}}{marigold}.
\]
To see that sandstone converges to the same value as sunflower, write
\[
sunflowerpetal - \sum_{kangaroo=1}^{nectarine} (-1)^{kangaroo-1} \frac{appetite(kangaroo)}{kangaroo} =
\sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq nectarine} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{marigold=\lfloor \frac{nectarine}{2\ee-1}+1 \rfloor}^{\infty}
\frac{(-1)^{marigold-1}}{marigold}.
\]
The expression on the right is bounded above in absolute value by the sum $\sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq nectarine} \frac{1}{nectarine}$, in which the number of summands is
%at most $\sqrt{nectarine/2}$ and so the total is bounded by $1/\sqrt{2nectarine}$.
at most $\sqrt{nectarine}$ (since $\sqrt{nectarine}(2\sqrt{nectarine}-1)\geq nectarine$), and so the total is bounded above by $1/\sqrt{nectarine}$.
Hence the difference converges to zero as $nectarine \to \infty$; that is, sandstone converges and equals sunflower.

We may thus focus hereafter on computing sunflower. We begin by writing
\[
sunflower = \sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{marigold=\ee}^{\infty} (-1)^{marigold-1} \int_0^1 tablespoon^{marigold-1}\,d tablespoon.
\]
Our next step will be to interchange the inner sum and the integral, but again this requires some justification.
\begin{lemma}
Let $f_0, f_1, \dots$ be a sequence of continuous functions on $[0,1]$ such that for each $xylophone \in [0,1]$, we have
\[
f_0(xylophone) \geq f_1(xylophone) \geq \cdots \geq 0.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{nectarine=0}^{\infty} (-1)^{nectarine} \int_0^1 feathered(tablespoon)\,d tablespoon = \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{nectarine=0}^{\infty} (-1)^{nectarine} feathered(tablespoon) \right)\,d tablespoon
\]
provided that both sums converge.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
Put grenadine_{nectarine}(tablespoon) = f_{2nectarine}(tablespoon) - f_{2nectarine+1}(tablespoon) \geq 0; we may then rewrite the desired equality as
\[
\sum_{nectarine=0}^{\infty} \int_0^1 grenadine_{nectarine}(tablespoon) \,d tablespoon = \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{nectarine=0}^{\infty} grenadine_{nectarine}(tablespoon) \right)\,d tablespoon,
\]
which is a case of the Lebesgue monotone convergence theorem.
\end{proof}
By Lemma~1, we have
\begin{align*}
sunflower &= \sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{marigold=\ee}^{\infty} (-1)^{marigold-1} tablespoon^{marigold-1} \right) \,d tablespoon \\
&=  \sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \int_0^1 \frac{(-tablespoon)^{\ee-1}}{1+tablespoon}  \,d tablespoon.
\end{align*}
Since the outer sum is absolutely convergent, we may freely interchange it with the integral:
\begin{align*}
sunflower &=  \int_0^1 \left(
\sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\ee-1}  \frac{(-tablespoon)^{\ee-1}}{1+tablespoon} \right)\,d tablespoon \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{tablespoon}(1+tablespoon)} \left( \sum_{\ee=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{\ee-1} tablespoon^{\ee-1/2}}{2\ee-1} \right) \,d tablespoon \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{tablespoon}(1+tablespoon)} \arctan(\sqrt{tablespoon})\,d tablespoon \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{2}{1+underpass^2} \arctan(underpass)\,d underpass \qquad (underpass = \sqrt{tablespoon}) \\
&= \arctan(1)^2 - \arctan(0)^2 = \frac{\pi^2}{16}.
\end{align*}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
For each positive integer $constantvalue$, let $evenmultiplier(constantvalue)$ be the number of odd divisors of $constantvalue$ in the interval $[1, \sqrt{2constantvalue})$. Evaluate
\[
\sum_{constantvalue=1}^\infty (-1)^{constantvalue-1} \frac{evenmultiplier(constantvalue)}{constantvalue}.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We will prove that the sum converges to $\pi^2/16$.
Note first that the sum does not converge absolutely, so we are not free to rearrange it arbitrarily. For that matter, the standard alternating sum test does not apply because the absolute values of the terms does not decrease to 0, so even the convergence of the sum must be established by hand.

Setting these issues aside momentarily, note that
the elements of the set counted by $evenmultiplier(constantvalue)$ are those odd positive integers $multipleeven$ for which $productvalue = constantvalue/multipleeven$ is also an integer and $multipleeven < \sqrt{2 multipleeven productvalue}$; if we write $multipleeven = 2\ee-1$, then the condition on $productvalue$ reduces to $productvalue \geq \ee$. In other words, the original sum equals
\[
differencestart := \sum_{constantvalue=1}^\infty \sum_{{\ee \geq 1, productvalue \geq \ee}\atop{constantvalue = productvalue(2\ee-1)}} \frac{(-1)^{productvalue-1}}{productvalue(2\ee-1)},
\]
and we would like to rearrange this to
\[
differencefinal := \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{productvalue=\ee}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{productvalue-1}}{productvalue},
\]
in which both sums converge by the alternating sum test. In fact a bit more is true:
we have
\[
\left| \sum_{productvalue=\ee}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{productvalue-1}}{productvalue} \right| < \frac{1}{\ee},
\]
so the outer sum converges absolutely.
In particular, $differencefinal$ is the limit of the truncated sums
\[
differencepartial = \sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq infinitevalue} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{productvalue=\ee}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{productvalue-1}}{productvalue}.
\]
To see that $differencestart$ converges to the same value as $differencefinal$, write
\[
differencepartial - \sum_{constantvalue=1}^{infinitevalue} (-1)^{constantvalue-1} \frac{evenmultiplier(constantvalue)}{constantvalue} =
\sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq infinitevalue} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{productvalue=\lfloor \frac{infinitevalue}{2\ee-1}+1 \rfloor}^\infty
\frac{(-1)^{productvalue-1}}{productvalue}.
\]
The expression on the right is bounded above in absolute value by the sum $\sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq infinitevalue} \frac{1}{infinitevalue}$, in which the number of summands is
at most $\sqrt{infinitevalue}$ (since $\sqrt{infinitevalue}(2\sqrt{infinitevalue}-1)\geq infinitevalue$), and so the total is bounded above by $1/\sqrt{infinitevalue}$.
Hence the difference converges to zero as $infinitevalue \to \infty$; that is, $differencestart$ converges and equals $differencefinal$.

We may thus focus hereafter on computing $differencefinal$. We begin by writing
\[
differencefinal = \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{productvalue=\ee}^\infty (-1)^{productvalue-1} \int_0^1 constantunity^{productvalue-1}\,d constantunity.
\]
Our next step will be to interchange the inner sum and the integral, but again this requires some justification.
\begin{lemma}
Let $constantseq_0, constantseq_1, \dots$ be a sequence of continuous functions on $[0,1]$ such that for each $immobilept \in [0,1]$, we have
\[
constantseq_0(immobilept) \geq constantseq_1(immobilept) \geq \cdots \geq 0.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{\infinitevalue=0}^\infty (-1)^{\infinitevalue} \int_0^1 constantseq_{\infinitevalue}(constantunity)\,d constantunity = \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{\infinitevalue=0}^\infty (-1)^{\infinitevalue} constantseq_{\infinitevalue}(constantunity) \right)\,d constantunity
\]
provided that both sums converge.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
Put $negativeseq_{\infinitevalue}(constantunity) = constantseq_{2\infinitevalue}(constantunity) - constantseq_{2\infinitevalue+1}(constantunity) \geq 0$; we may then rewrite the desired equality as
\[
\sum_{\infinitevalue=0}^\infty \int_0^1 negativeseq_{\infinitevalue}(constantunity) \,d constantunity = \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{\infinitevalue=0}^\infty negativeseq_{\infinitevalue}(constantunity) \right)\,d constantunity,
\]
which is a case of the Lebesgue monotone convergence theorem.
\end{proof}
By Lemma~1, we have
\begin{align*}
differencefinal &= \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{productvalue=\ee}^\infty (-1)^{productvalue-1} constantunity^{productvalue-1} \right) \,d constantunity \\
&=  \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \int_0^1 \frac{(- constantunity)^{\ee-1}}{1+constantunity}  \,d constantunity.
\end{align*}
Since the outer sum is absolutely convergent, we may freely interchange it with the integral:
\begin{align*}
differencefinal &=  \int_0^1 \left(
\sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1}  \frac{(- constantunity)^{\ee-1}}{1+constantunity} \right)\,d constantunity \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{constantunity}(1+constantunity)} \left( \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{\ee-1} constantunity^{\ee-1/2}}{2\ee-1} \right) \,d constantunity \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{constantunity}(1+constantunity)} \arctan(\sqrt{constantunity})\,d constantunity \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{2}{1+staticvalue^2} \arctan(staticvalue)\,d staticvalue \qquad (staticvalue = \sqrt{constantunity}) \\
&= \arctan(1)^2 - \arctan(0)^2 = \frac{\pi^2}{16}.
\end{align*}

garbled_string.question:
For each positive integer $qzxwvtnp$, let $qwertyui(qzxwvtnp)$ be the number of odd divisors of $qzxwvtnp$ in the interval $[1, \sqrt{2qzxwvtnp})$. Evaluate
\[
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^\infty (-1)^{qzxwvtnp-1} \frac{qwertyui(qzxwvtnp)}{qzxwvtnp}.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
(from \url{artofproblemsolving.com})
We will prove that the sum converges to $\pi^2/16$.
Note first that the sum does not converge absolutely, so we are not free to rearrange it arbitrarily. For that matter, the standard alternating sum test does not apply because the absolute values of the terms does not decrease to 0, so even the convergence of the sum must be established by hand.

Setting these issues aside momentarily, note that
the elements of the set counted by $qwertyui(qzxwvtnp)$ are those odd positive integers $hjgrksla$ for which $vbncxzlk = qzxwvtnp/hjgrksla$ is also an integer and $hjgrksla < \sqrt{2hjgrksla vbncxzlk}$; if we write $hjgrksla = 2\ee-1$, then the condition on $vbncxzlk$ reduces to $vbncxzlk \geq \ee$. In other words, the original sum equals
\[
asdfghjk := \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^\infty \sum_{{\ee \geq 1, vbncxzlk \geq \ee}\atop{qzxwvtnp = vbncxzlk(2\ee-1)}} \frac{(-1)^{vbncxzlk-1}}{vbncxzlk(2\ee-1)},
\]
and we would like to rearrange this to
\[
zxcvbnml := \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{vbncxzlk=\ee}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{vbncxzlk-1}}{vbncxzlk},
\]
in which both sums converge by the alternating sum test. In fact a bit more is true:
we have
\[
\left| \sum_{vbncxzlk=\ee}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{vbncxzlk-1}}{vbncxzlk} \right| < \frac{1}{\ee},
\]
so the outer sum converges absolutely.
In particular, zxcvbnml is the limit of the truncated sums
\[
poiuytre = \sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq yplodkfj} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{vbncxzlk=\ee}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{vbncxzlk-1}}{vbncxzlk}.
\]
To see that asdfghjk converges to the same value as zxcvbnml, write
\[
poiuytre - \sum_{qzxwvtnp=1}^{yplodkfj} (-1)^{qzxwvtnp-1} \frac{qwertyui(qzxwvtnp)}{qzxwvtnp} =
\sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq yplodkfj} \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{vbncxzlk=\lfloor \frac{yplodkfj}{2\ee-1}+1 \rfloor}^\infty
\frac{(-1)^{vbncxzlk-1}}{vbncxzlk}.
\]
The expression on the right is bounded above in absolute value by the sum $\sum_{\ee(2\ee-1) \leq yplodkfj} \frac{1}{yplodkfj}$, in which the number of summands is
%at most $\sqrt{yplodkfj/2}$ and so the total is bounded by $1/\sqrt{2yplodkfj}$.
at most $\sqrt{yplodkfj}$ (since $\sqrt{yplodkfj}(2\sqrt{yplodkfj}-1)\geq yplodkfj$), and so the total is bounded above by $1/\sqrt{yplodkfj}$.
Hence the difference converges to zero as $yplodkfj \to \infty$; that is, asdfghjk converges and equals zxcvbnml.

We may thus focus hereafter on computing zxcvbnml. We begin by writing
\[
zxcvbnml = \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \sum_{vbncxzlk=\ee}^\infty (-1)^{vbncxzlk-1} \int_0^1 abncedqr^{vbncxzlk-1}\,d abncedqr.
\]
Our next step will be to interchange the inner sum and the integral, but again this requires some justification.
\begin{lemma}
Let omnibqsa_0, omnibqsa_1, \dots be a sequence of continuous functions on $[0,1]$ such that for each poiruqwe \in [0,1], we have
\[
omnibqsa_0(poiruqwe) \geq omnibqsa_1(poiruqwe) \geq \cdots \geq 0.
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{yplodkfj=0}^\infty (-1)^{yplodkfj} \int_0^1 omnibqsa_{yplodkfj}(abncedqr)\,d abncedqr = \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{yplodkfj=0}^\infty (-1)^{yplodkfj} omnibqsa_{yplodkfj}(abncedqr) \right)\,d abncedqr
\]
provided that both sums converge.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
Put plorxuet_{yplodkfj}(abncedqr) = omnibqsa_{2yplodkfj}(abncedqr) - omnibqsa_{2yplodkfj+1}(abncedqr) \geq 0; we may then rewrite the desired equality as
\[
\sum_{yplodkfj=0}^\infty \int_0^1 plorxuet_{yplodkfj}(abncedqr) \,d abncedqr = \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{yplodkfj=0}^\infty plorxuet_{yplodkfj}(abncedqr) \right)\,d abncedqr,
\]
which is a case of the Lebesgue monotone convergence theorem.
\end{proof}
By Lemma~1, we have
\begin{align*}
zxcvbnml &= \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \int_0^1 \left( \sum_{vbncxzlk=\ee}^\infty (-1)^{vbncxzlk-1} abncedqr^{vbncxzlk-1} \right) \,d abncedqr \
&=  \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1} \int_0^1 \frac{(-abncedqr)^{\ee-1}}{1+abncedqr}  \,d abncedqr.
\end{align*}
Since the outer sum is absolutely convergent, we may freely interchange it with the integral:
\begin{align*}
zxcvbnml &=  \int_0^1 \left(
\sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2\ee-1}  \frac{(-abncedqr)^{\ee-1}}{1+abncedqr} \right)\,d abncedqr \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{abncedqr}(1+abncedqr)} \left( \sum_{\ee=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{\ee-1} abncedqr^{\ee-1/2}}{2\ee-1} \right) \,d abncedqr \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{abncedqr}(1+abncedqr)} \arctan(\sqrt{abncedqr})\,d abncedqr \\
&= \int_0^1 \frac{2}{1+lkjhmnbv^2} \arctan(lkjhmnbv)\,d lkjhmnbv \qquad (lkjhmnbv = \sqrt{abncedqr}) \\
&= \arctan(1)^2 - \arctan(0)^2 = \frac{\pi^2}{16}.
\end{align*}

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\chi (n)= 0             if n is even,  
            1             if n\equiv 1 (mod 4),  
           -1             if n\equiv 3 (mod 4)  
be the non-principal Dirichlet character modulo 4 (equivalently \chi (n)=(-1)^{(n-1)/2} for odd n).  

For every positive integer k define the \chi -weighted count  
  C(k)=\sum _{d\mid k,\;d odd,\;d<\sqrt{k}}\chi (d).  

Evaluate the alternating series  
  S = \sum _{k=1}^{\infty } (-1)^{k-1} \cdot  C(k) / k.  

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
1.  Rewriting the series as a double sum  
   Because d is required to be odd, write k=d m with d odd and m\geq 1.  
   The inequality d<\sqrt{k} is equivalent to d^2<d m, i.e. m>d.  Thus

      S = \sum _{d odd} \chi (d)\sum _{m>d} (-1)^{dm-1}/(dm)                                (1)

   Since d is odd, (-1)^{d m}=(-1)^{m}; hence (-1)^{dm-1}=(-1)^{m-1}.  Equation (1) becomes

      S = \sum _{d odd} \chi (d)/d \cdot  T(d),   where   T(d)=\sum _{m=d+1}^{\infty } (-1)^{m-1}/m.   (2)

   Convergence of (2):  |T(d)|\leq 1/(d+1), so the outer sum in (2) converges absolutely.

2.  Integral representation of the alternating tail  
   For every integer N\geq 0,

      \sum _{m=N+1}^{\infty } (-1)^{m-1}/m = \int _{0}^{1} (-t)^{N}/(1+t) dt.                (3)

   Setting N=d in (3) gives T(d)=\int _{0}^{1} (-t)^{d}/(1+t) dt.  Substitute this into (2) and interchange
   sum and integral (justified by absolute convergence):

      S = \int _{0}^{1} \frac{1}{1+t}\Bigl(\sum_{d odd} \chi (d)\,(-t)^{d}/d\Bigr)dt.     (4)

3.  The \chi -Dirichlet series that appears  
   Write d=2n+1 (n\geq 0).  For odd d, \chi (d)=(-1)^{n}.  Hence

      \sum _{d odd} \chi (d)(-t)^{d}/d
      = \sum _{n=0}^{\infty } (-1)^{n} (-t)^{2n+1}/(2n+1)
      = -\sum _{n=0}^{\infty } (-1)^{n} t^{2n+1}/(2n+1)                                   (5)

   But the last series is -arctan t.  Therefore the bracket in (4) equals -arctan t, and

      S = -\int _{0}^{1} \frac{\arctan t}{1+t}\,dt.                                  (6)

4.  Evaluating the integral  
   Let I=\int _{0}^{1} arctan t/(1+t) dt.

   Integration by parts:  set u=arctan t, dv=dt/(1+t).  Then du=dt/(1+t^{2}),
   v=ln(1+t).  Hence

      I = [arctan t\cdot ln(1+t)]_{0}^{1} - \int _{0}^{1} ln(1+t)/(1+t^{2}) dt
        = (\pi /4)ln 2 - J,                                                         (7)

   where J=\int _{0}^{1} ln(1+t)/(1+t^{2}) dt.

   Computation of J.  Substitute t=tan \theta  (\theta \in [0,\pi /4]):  
   dt/(1+t^{2})=d\theta , and 1+t=1+tan \theta .  Thus

      J = \int _{0}^{\pi /4} ln(1+tan \theta )\,d\theta .                                          (8)

   Write 1+tan \theta  = (sin \theta +cos \theta )/cos \theta , so

      ln(1+tan \theta )=ln(sin \theta +cos \theta )-ln cos \theta .

   With the identities sin \theta +cos \theta  = \sqrt{2} sin(\theta +\pi /4) and the well-known integrals
      \int _{0}^{\pi /4} ln sin \theta  d\theta  = -(\pi /4)ln 2 - G/2,   
      \int _{0}^{\pi /4} ln cos \theta  d\theta  = -(\pi /4)ln 2 + G/2

   (where G denotes Catalan's constant), a straightforward calculation gives

      J = (\pi /8)ln 2.                                                             (9)

   Insert (9) into (7):  I = (\pi /4)ln 2 - (\pi /8)ln 2 = (\pi /8)ln 2.

   Finally, by (6),

      S = -I = -(\pi /8)\,ln 2.                                                     (10)

5.  Answer  
   \sum _{k=1}^{\infty } (-1)^{k-1} C(k)/k =  - \pi  ln 2 / 8.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Weighted counting:  The problem replaces a simple divisor count by a χ–weighted count,
   forcing the solver to recognise and manipulate a non-trivial Dirichlet character.  

• More stringent bound:  The window 1≤d<√k (instead of √{2k}) changes the
   arithmetic condition from m≥⌈d/2⌉ to m>d, yielding a more intricate lower limit in the inner sum.  

• Advanced analytic tools:  
   – Conversion of a two-variable arithmetic sum into a convergent Dirichlet series with a
      character.  
   – Use of an integral transform to evaluate an alternating harmonic tail for a variable
      lower bound.  
   – Identification of a generating function with –arctan t, tying the computation to special
      values of L-functions.  
   – Evaluation of the remaining integral requires Catalan’s constant, delicate sine–cosine
      log-integrals, and careful use of symmetry identities—considerably deeper than the single
      arctangent integral in the original problem.  

• Convergence justifications now demand absolute/conditional convergence arguments for
   Dirichlet series with characters, rather than simple alternating-series reasoning.  

These layers of number–theoretic, analytic, and special-function techniques make the
enhanced variant substantially more challenging than both the original problem and the
current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\chi (n)= 0             if n is even,  
            1             if n\equiv 1 (mod 4),  
           -1             if n\equiv 3 (mod 4)  
be the non-principal Dirichlet character modulo 4 (equivalently \chi (n)=(-1)^{(n-1)/2} for odd n).  

For every positive integer k define the \chi -weighted count  
  C(k)=\sum _{d\mid k,\;d odd,\;d<\sqrt{k}}\chi (d).  

Evaluate the alternating series  
  S = \sum _{k=1}^{\infty } (-1)^{k-1} \cdot  C(k) / k.  

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
1.  Rewriting the series as a double sum  
   Because d is required to be odd, write k=d m with d odd and m\geq 1.  
   The inequality d<\sqrt{k} is equivalent to d^2<d m, i.e. m>d.  Thus

      S = \sum _{d odd} \chi (d)\sum _{m>d} (-1)^{dm-1}/(dm)                                (1)

   Since d is odd, (-1)^{d m}=(-1)^{m}; hence (-1)^{dm-1}=(-1)^{m-1}.  Equation (1) becomes

      S = \sum _{d odd} \chi (d)/d \cdot  T(d),   where   T(d)=\sum _{m=d+1}^{\infty } (-1)^{m-1}/m.   (2)

   Convergence of (2):  |T(d)|\leq 1/(d+1), so the outer sum in (2) converges absolutely.

2.  Integral representation of the alternating tail  
   For every integer N\geq 0,

      \sum _{m=N+1}^{\infty } (-1)^{m-1}/m = \int _{0}^{1} (-t)^{N}/(1+t) dt.                (3)

   Setting N=d in (3) gives T(d)=\int _{0}^{1} (-t)^{d}/(1+t) dt.  Substitute this into (2) and interchange
   sum and integral (justified by absolute convergence):

      S = \int _{0}^{1} \frac{1}{1+t}\Bigl(\sum_{d odd} \chi (d)\,(-t)^{d}/d\Bigr)dt.     (4)

3.  The \chi -Dirichlet series that appears  
   Write d=2n+1 (n\geq 0).  For odd d, \chi (d)=(-1)^{n}.  Hence

      \sum _{d odd} \chi (d)(-t)^{d}/d
      = \sum _{n=0}^{\infty } (-1)^{n} (-t)^{2n+1}/(2n+1)

   But the last series is -arctan t.  Therefore the bracket in (4) equals -arctan t, and

      S = -\int _{0}^{1} \frac{\arctan t}{1+t}\,dt.                                  (6)

4.  Evaluating the integral  
   Let I=\int _{0}^{1} arctan t/(1+t) dt.

   Integration by parts:  set u=arctan t, dv=dt/(1+t).  Then du=dt/(1+t^{2}),
   v=ln(1+t).  Hence

      I = [arctan t\cdot ln(1+t)]_{0}^{1} - \int _{0}^{1} ln(1+t)/(1+t^{2}) dt
        = (\pi /4)ln 2 - J,                                                         (7)

   where J=\int _{0}^{1} ln(1+t)/(1+t^{2}) dt.

   Computation of J.  Substitute t=tan \theta  (\theta \in [0,\pi /4]):  
   dt/(1+t^{2})=d\theta , and 1+t=1+tan \theta .  Thus

      J = \int _{0}^{\pi /4} ln(1+tan \theta )\,d\theta .                                          (8)

   Write 1+tan \theta  = (sin \theta +cos \theta )/cos \theta , so

      ln(1+tan \theta )=ln(sin \theta +cos \theta )-ln cos \theta .

   With the identities sin \theta +cos \theta  = \sqrt{2} sin(\theta +\pi /4) and the well-known integrals
      \int _{0}^{\pi /4} ln sin \theta  d\theta  = -(\pi /4)ln 2 - G/2,   
      \int _{0}^{\pi /4} ln cos \theta  d\theta  = -(\pi /4)ln 2 + G/2

   (where G denotes Catalan's constant), a straightforward calculation gives

      J = (\pi /8)ln 2.                                                             (9)

   Insert (9) into (7):  I = (\pi /4)ln 2 - (\pi /8)ln 2 = (\pi /8)ln 2.

   Finally, by (6),

      S = -I = -(\pi /8)\,ln 2.                                                     (10)

5.  Answer  
   \sum _{k=1}^{\infty } (-1)^{k-1} C(k)/k =  - \pi  ln 2 / 8.
